item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think 2.5 billion years is stretching credulity a bit. Maybe 2.5 million?

Based on current observations SCP-2180 doesn't does not take place in any specific location or time during the era, and has contains fauna from different continents.

I liked how you wove in the theory about 106 being a predator of early humans, but then you made it be a post-apocalyptic future, which has been overdone.

No vote for now.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: No complaints from here.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Timer expired. Edits made as discussed below. The author may revert at any time.

+ show block
– hide block

The author explicitly states they make reference to SCP-106 and SCP-217 up thread, which could easily be made into a few inline links. As such, I am suggesting the following:

Editing this:

10/18/████ - 7:03 AM: A gold colored Glyptodon enters the forest from the right side of the painting, after moving through the field. Animals avoid the creature. This is the first observation of colors not used in Paleolithic art.

To this:

10/18/████ - 7:03 AM: A gold colored Glyptodon enters the forest from the right side of the painting, after moving through the field. Animals avoid the creature. This is the first observation of colors not used in Paleolithic art.

Editing this:

11/3/████ - 2:57 AM: A black circle appears underneath one of the sleeping field humans, and a black humanoid figure steps out of it. The figure grabs the human and pulls him into the circle. Following this the circle fades away.

To this:

11/3/████ - 2:57 AM: A black circle appears underneath one of the sleeping field humans, and a black humanoid figure steps out of it. The figure grabs the human and pulls him into the circle. Following this the circle fades away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think it needs clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I think it needs clarification.

Oops, wrong thread; sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The latter. If you think that needs some clarification I can add that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the left side of SCP-2180 is a mountain, with a network of caves inside it. Located in one of the deeper areas of the cave is the number "2067," which is etched onto the cave wall in Arabic numerals.

So an observer can "zoom" their view into cave and navigate through it? Or does the painting show multiple cross-sections of the cave?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, not too keen on those so it was easy to overlook the references. In any case, I do love the mystery and general eeriness surrounding this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only scips intentionally referenced are SCP-106 and SCP-217. The K class scenario is meant to be left ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cant help but feel missing out on an important detail here. Is there a related scip in here Im not aware of or is the painting depicting an ambiguous k class scenario?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea but there are enough flaws that I can't upvote yet. My first thought was, "oh it's yet another sentient drawing, yawn". It took until the event log before I realized that it's more of a recording. Instead of "the organisms are animate", I would suggest "the painting is animate".

Typo: missing "in which", first sentence

SCP section is just bad. Why do we have an MTF guarding a super safe painting? What does it even mean to close off the rest of the cave system? Why is there a list of cover stories?

"Cobalt fire" doesn't really parse correctly. Is it a blue fire, or the fire you get from burning cobalt? If the former, just say "blue".

Overall my criticism boils down to "be more clinical".
http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/clinical-tone-declassified

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Infected with SCP-217, I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, but am not sure what was going on with the Glyptodon. Is it a tank or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it well enough until the painting started referencing the Foundation. Many articles utilize this "twist" i tend to find it fairly cliche.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
11/3/████ - 2:57 AM: A black circle appears underneath one of the sleeping field humans, and a black humanoid figure steps out of it. The figure grabs the human and pulls him into the circle. Following this the circle fades away.

1: Good work linking to a well-known skip in a non-cheesy way.
2: That was rather perfectly timed to start introducing anomalies into the story.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The addendum is the strength of this article - it tells a really nice story. However, that Description section could be much improved, right down to the level of grammar. Examples that come to mind first: it should be "Arabic numerals", with a capital A; that semicolon in the list of behaviours doesn't belong there; you need to think about comma usage generally - for example, I'd personally say "and, depending on [the] species, hunt".

And there's plenty of scope for zhooshing it up with more precise, technical language. Saying "the top quarter has a sun and moon" isn't great. Something like "[figures/forms/illustrations] [corresponding to/analogous to/similar in appearance and behaviour to] Earth's Sun and Moon [can be observed/are depicted/are present]" would be better tonally. Remember: on this site, you can't be too anal!

The addendum is already pretty great, but there's still room for improvement. (Warning: nitpicky, probably plain-wrong section incoming.) I feel that sprinkling in a few more details about the flames and the navy humanoids would make the reader fear them more. It's a fine line, obviously, because you need to leave some mystery… (Am I missing some reference to another scip?) A few more details throughout might be nice. Also, you might want to experiment with some tried-and-tested storytelling devices to make us identify with the "goodies" even more. e.g. what if you identify one of the figures as having distinctive red hair, and then establish them as a heroic character with some suitably heroic gesture, and then they're the one who's tragically killed in battle with the Smilodon? That sort of thing.

But on the whole: neat idea, nicely implemented - just work on that Description, please!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eight purple ovals appear around the moon.
He flips to a page about █K scenario.

So,
It's possible that I'm too into night vale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some things that could be ironed out still, but upvoting because I believe in you. This is the kind of thing that's been done before on this site, yet making it a cave painting adds so much to the base concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the addendum there were a couple of sentences where the words were in the wrong order so I fixed those, but other than that, I enjoyed this. There are a couple of parts where the tone slips a little, and some of the wording is a bit awkward, but the concept, while simple, is really interesting and executed nicely. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solid work on the addenda. You cross link in a wonderfully subtle way that ties your own unique bits together naturally, without relying solely on the references.

You do an excellent job of building up a sense of unsettling Wrongness throughout the log, which is (for me) dispelled with the direct Foundation references. I feel like it could be toned down a tad to better jive with the rest. +1 regardless, because I was still pretty creeped out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a mystery!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woah, I was not expecting the painting to be 2.5 billion years old. I like the idea of a living painting. Though, one question. How is a painting of prehistoric humans so old?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I think this concept isn't bad. I'm not saying I love it, but it's okay.

A few scientific innaccuraccies to nitpick at:
We can U-series date cave paintings back millions of years http://science.sciencemag.org/content/336/6087/1409 as seen in this article.

Solved!

Secondly, arabic numerals definitely didn't exist in the paleolithic era, which I'm assuming this is supposed to date from. Welp, I'll give you that one, 2.5 billion works.

Smilodon was from the Pleistocene epoch, which includes the paleolithic period, but without a period attached to this, it's hard to determine the accuracy of this.

I'm not a fan of the idea of not being able to affect the paint in any way, as it seems like a cop out from the harder science of the peice, by covering it up with being unable to touch it.

Other than that, the tone of the description is a little off, with imagery being used, and phrases like "generalized version of things present during the era" which is honestly a two million year period that we don't particularly have a complete record, supposedly squashed in to a 160m^2 area.

The observation log feels off, tone wise as well, with things like describing objects in the abstract (berry farm, socialization "taking place" over a specific time)

As a general critique, this isn't a particularly grabbing article. There's some interesting images, in the log, but nothing unexpected.

I think this is fairly well thought out, but the execution needs work.

Edit: After reviewing some of the changes, my vote is changing from downvote to upvote. Thank you for addressing the inconsistencies. That one small change (dating to 2.5 billion years) really changed the tone of the article completely.
